perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made without the consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to comply with the terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit requirement to return materials by a certain date and prohibiting unauthorized copies weakens the protection of confidential information. It introduces a legal risk, particularly if the confidential information involves trade secrets. Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing specific requirements regarding the return and copying of confidential information, the modified text weakens the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under Virginia law. The VUTSA requires trade secret holders to take active steps to prevent unauthorized disclosure, and this omission could be seen as a failure to do so.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-337 . Injunctive relief. A. Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. B. In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. C. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 1986, c. 210. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Unc
perturbation.justification: The omission of specific requirements regarding the return and copying of confidential information in the modified text weakens the 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under Virginia law. The original text explicitly states that removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date and that no copies are to be made without the consent of the Disclosing Party. The changed text removes these specific protective measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 59.1-337, pertains to injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the obligation to return materials and prohibiting unauthorized copies, the modified contract undermines the ability to prevent misappropriation, making it more difficult to obtain injunctive relief. Therefore, the omission creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof; provided, however, Receiving Party’s legal department or outside legal counsel may maintain a single copy of Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for purposes of regulatory compliance and compliance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of the clause allowing the Receiving Party's legal department or outside legal counsel to retain a copy for regulatory compliance could be problematic. Companies have a legal obligation to maintain records for compliance purposes. The omission could force a party to destroy documents needed for compliance, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: This clause omits the Receiving Party's right to retain copies for compliance purposes. 18 U.S. Code § 1519 prohibits the destruction or alteration of records with the intent to impede or influence a federal investigation. While not directly compelling retention, this clause makes it difficult for the Receiving Party to comply with legal requirements, as destroying confidential materials that could be used in a theoretical future legal case could be seen as breaking compliance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit allowance for the Receiving Party's legal department or counsel to retain a copy of Confidential Information for regulatory compliance. This omission creates a potential conflict with 18 U.S. Code § 1519, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the original contract allowed for document retention for compliance, the modified contract lacks this provision, potentially forcing the Receiving Party to destroy documents. The 'Destruction, alteration, or falsification of records' language in 18 USC 1519 could be interpreted as being violated if the Receiving Party destroys documents that they are legally obligated to retain for compliance purposes due to the new contract. The scraped snippet 1 explicitly references the destruction of records in the context of federal investigations, and the removal of the clause allowing retention could force destruction of the Confidential Information.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that (a) the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information may represent material, non-public information of the Disclosing Party; (b) federal securities laws prohibit anyone who is in possession of material, non-public information from purchasing or selling the Disclosing Party’s securities on the basis of material, non-public information of the Disclosing Party; and (c) each Party shall comply with all applicable securities laws and regulations with respect to material, non-public information of the Disclosing Party disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that (a) the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information may represent material, non-public information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit statements about insider trading and compliance with securities laws creates a significant risk. The contract fails to adequately inform the receiving party of their obligations under federal securities laws. The SEC Rule 10b-5 prohibits insider trading. By omitting key clauses, the contract is misleading.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of clauses explicitly referencing the prohibition of insider trading and the obligation to comply with securities laws, directly contradicts the SEC's regulations against insider trading outlined in 17 CFR § 240.10b-5. By removing the acknowledgement of these obligations, the agreement may not adequately inform all parties of their legal duties, increasing the risk of violations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . perturbation.justification: The omission of clauses referencing the prohibition of insider trading and compliance with securities laws directly contradicts SEC Rule 10b-5, which prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original contract explicitly acknowledges the prohibition and the obligation to comply. By removing these acknowledgements, the changed text fails to adequately inform parties of their legal duties, increasing the risk of violations and creating a misleading impression regarding insider trading restrictions, which goes against 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 (b): 'to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading.'

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, subject to the terms and conditions imposed on the removal of Confidential Information under Paragraph 6, sets forth the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and it may not be modified or amended except by a written agreement executed by the parties hereto; provided, however, (a) each existing confidentiality agreement between some or all of the Parties shall survive the execution of this Agreement and continue in full force and effect in accordance with its stated terms and conditions, and (b) the confidentiality obligations arising under the United States Collaboration Agreement, dated March 31, 2005, by and among Shire LLC, Shire Pharmaceuticals Group PLC, and New River Pharmaceuticals Inc., or under the ROW Territory License Agreement, dated March 31, 2005, by and among Shire Pharmaceuticals Ireland Limited, Shire Pharmaceuticals Group PLC, and New River Pharmaceuticals Inc., shall survive the execution of this Agreement and continue in full force and effect in accordance with the stated terms and conditions of each.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and it may not be modified or amended except by a written agreement executed by the parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that existing confidentiality agreements and specific collaboration/license agreements survive introduces legal risk. Failing to acknowledge the continued validity of prior agreements could create ambiguity and potentially violate the terms of those pre-existing legally binding documents, particularly concerning intellectual property rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the 'provided, however' clause creates the potential to breach the existing agreements. By failing to explicitly acknowledge the survival of these agreements, the new contract arguably violates the principle of honoring prior contractual obligations, potentially creating a breach of contract under general contract law principles codified in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 209, concerning integrated agreements. It does not explicitly void the other contracts, but an ambiguous contract might violate the intentions of the earlier contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (

perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'provided, however' clause creates a contradiction because it fails to explicitly acknowledge the survival of existing agreements. This contradicts the principle of honoring prior contractual obligations. The first snippet explains that a contract creates 'mutual obligations that are enforceable by law,' and that private law (i.e. the private agreement) includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. The changed agreement undermines the previous agreements. By removing the clause which acknowledges the survival of the older agreements, it fails to honor mutual obligations that would be legally enforceable.
